questions.question: Rimmed steel ingot practice is preferred for low-carbon sheet products because:
options: full deoxidation with silicon and aluminum gives the ingot uniform chemistry from rim to core
vigorous carbon monoxide evolution during freezing forms a rim of nearly pure iron with an excellent surface — ideal for sheet — while blowholes and segregation are confined to the interior
the rimming action eliminates all gas porosity, leaving only a small, easily cropped pipe
rimmed ingots demand the deepest hot tops, which improve sheet surface quality
questions.explanation: Rimmed steel is barely deoxidized, so CO evolves vigorously as the ingot freezes, stirring the mold and freezing a rim of nearly pure iron with excellent surface quality over a ring of blowholes and a strongly segregated core — with little or no pipe. The practice is workable only below roughly 0.25% carbon. Full deoxidation and uniform chemistry describe killed steel, which pays for its quiet solidification with the deepest pipe — killed ingots, not rimmed, are the ones managed with hot tops and cropping.

questions.question: During magnetic particle testing of hot-rolled steel bar, tight longitudinal surface indications are evaluated as seams. Seams in wrought bar originate from:
options: subsurface blowholes and ingot surface cracks whose oxidized walls could not weld shut during rolling, drawn out along the bar's length
clusters of nonmetallic inclusions elongated into internal stringers
uncropped ingot pipe flattened into a planar mid-thickness separation
overfills and fins folded into the surface at shallow angles during a rolling pass
questions.explanation: Hot working welds internal voids shut only when their surfaces are clean and unoxidized; blowholes and ingot surface cracks whose walls have oxidized cannot fuse, and rolling draws them out into tight longitudinal seams — prime targets for MT and eddy current on bars and tubing. Elongated inclusion clusters become stringers (internal), flattened pipe becomes a lamination at a plate's mid-thickness (internal and planar), and folded overfills are laps — a distinct surface discontinuity lying at shallow angles to the surface.

questions.question: A Level II is planning inspections for a sand-cast housing and a hot-rolled plate of the same alloy. Applying the principle that the process chooses the discontinuity, the correct expectation is:
options: both parts carry identical discontinuity populations, because alloy chemistry alone determines the flaws
the plate cannot contain internal discontinuities, because rolling welds every void shut
the casting's flaws are planar and directional, so it should be scanned ultrasonically with the grain flow in mind

the casting's flaws are volumetric and randomly oriented — radiography's home ground, graded against reference radiographs — while the plate's are planar and directional, favoring UT, MT, and ET planned around the rolling direction
questions.explanation: A casting's flaws are exactly as solidified: shrinkage, gas, and inclusions are volumetric and randomly oriented, which is why casting acceptance is organized around radiography graded against reference radiographs such as ASTM E155. Rolling flattens and draws ingot flaws into laminations, seams, and stringers — planar, directional discontinuities that UT, MT, and ET find when the scan plan respects the grain flow. Rolling welds shut only clean, unoxidized voids, so the plate is not flaw-free — and a casting, never having been worked, has no grain flow at all.

questions.question: A radiograph of a GMAW weld made in low-heat short-circuiting transfer is most likely to show the process's signature discontinuity:
options: tungsten inclusions printing as sharply defined light spots
slag inclusions trailing along incompletely cleaned passes
lack of fusion (cold lap) where the puddle failed to melt the joint sidewall
a missed seam where the beam wandered off the joint line
questions.explanation: GMAW feeds a continuous solid wire under external shielding gas — there is no flux and therefore no slag system — and in low-heat short-circuiting transfer the puddle can fail to melt the joint sidewall, making lack of fusion the GMAW signature. Tungsten inclusions belong to GTAW's nonconsumable electrode, slag inclusions to the flux-bearing processes (SMAW, FCAW, SAW), and missed seams to the very narrow electron beam and laser welds.

questions.question: A specification requires a 48-hour wait between completing welds in a susceptible high-strength steel and performing the final magnetic particle inspection. The waiting period exists because:
options: residual magnetism from the welding arc must decay before MT can be performed
the HAZ remains austenitic — and therefore nonmagnetic — for about two days after welding
natural stress relief during the wait opens cracks wider, making them easier to detect
hydrogen (underbead) cracking in the hardened HAZ can be delayed for hours or days after welding — inspecting too early certifies a weld that may crack afterward
questions.explanation: Atomic hydrogen absorbed during welding diffuses slowly, and in a HAZ quenched to hard, brittle untempered martensite it can trigger underbead and toe cracking long after the arc stops — hours or days. The waiting period lets any delayed cracks form before the accept/reject decision is made. Arc-induced magnetism is handled by demagnetization, not waiting; the HAZ transforms as it cools, not days later; and stress relief is a controlled furnace operation, not a passive benefit of sitting on a shelf.

questions.question: Radiography of a furnace-brazed assembly shows extensive voids and lack of fill. Investigation reveals the joint clearance was far above the specified 0.001–0.005 in. The connection between the two findings is:
options: an oversized clearance prevents the molten filler from entering the joint at all
capillary action collapses in an over-wide joint, so the filler is not drawn through the faying area — leaving voids and a weak joint limited to the filler metal's own strength
a wide clearance increases capillary attraction, so the voids must instead be flux entrapment
clearance does not affect filler distribution; the voids indicate the base metal melted locally
questions.explanation: Capillary action — the force that distributes brazing filler — is strongest in close-fitting joints, typically about 0.025–0.13 mm (0.001–0.005 in.) at brazing temperature. Too tight and the filler cannot flow; too open and the capillary force collapses, leaving voids and a filler-strength joint. A brazed joint develops strength from wetted area, which is why braze NDT maps how much of the faying surface is actually joined — and the base metal never melts in brazing at all.

questions.question: A precision tube is finished by cold drawing rather than hot working. Compared with hot-worked product, the cold-drawn tube can be expected to show:
options: higher strength and hardness with reduced ductility, excellent surface finish and tight tolerances — and stored residual stresses
no accumulated strain hardening, because new grains form as fast as the metal deforms
oxide scale and a decarburized surface layer
unlimited capacity for further deformation without any intermediate treatment
questions.explanation: Below the recrystallization temperature, dislocations multiply and tangle faster than the metal can heal, so cold work strain hardens: strength and hardness climb, ductility falls, and continued deformation eventually demands an intermediate anneal or the metal cracks. What cold work buys is precision — finish, tolerance, thin sections — at the price of residual stress and directional properties. Continuous recrystallization, scale, and decarburization are hot-working traits, where forces stay low but tolerances run loose.

questions.question: A high-strength steel component requires both fluorescent penetrant inspection and shot peening. Correct sequencing places the FPI:
options: after peening, because the dimpled surface retains penetrant better
at any convenient point, since peening changes only residual stress, not crack openings
before peening — or after a chemical etch if peening has already been done — because the overlapping impacts plastically smear the surface and can close crack openings against penetrant entry
only after the hydrogen embrittlement relief bake, which opens any cracks for detection
questions.explanation: Shot peening and grit blasting hammer and smear the surface, and smeared metal can seal fine crack openings so penetrant never enters. Penetrant inspection therefore belongs before peening, or after an etch reopens what the hammering shut — the same logic that puts final FPI at the bare-metal stage, before plating, paint, or anodic films mask the surface. The relief bake prevents delayed hydrogen cracking after plating; it does not open existing cracks.

questions.question: Which composite laminate defect is largely invisible to NDT after cure — making its prevention an in-process hold point rather than a post-cure inspection finding?
options: distributed porosity from a vacuum-bag leak during cure
a delamination from drilling damage at a fastener hole
a piece of backing film left between plies
wrong ply count or ply orientation
questions.explanation: A ply laid at the wrong angle literally changes the material — laminate properties are directional, built by stacking plies at 0°, ±45°, and 90° — yet after cure the error is largely undetectable by NDT. That is why ply count and orientation are verified against the ply book before bagging, as an in-process hold point. Porosity, delaminations, and foreign-object inclusions such as backing film are planar or volumetric absences of sound material that pulse-echo and through-transmission ultrasonics are well suited to find.

questions.question: Under Taylor's principle for fixed-limit gauging, a go/no-go gauge pair is designed so that:
options: the GO member is full form at the maximum-material limit and must enter or assemble — checking size and form together, the way the mating part will — while the NO-GO member, at the least-material limit, checks each dimension point by point and must not enter
the GO member is made at the least-material limit and must not enter, while the NO-GO member must assemble freely
both members display a numerical reading that can be recorded and trended
the GO member checks dimensions point by point while the NO-GO member is the full-form check
questions.explanation: Taylor's principle assigns the GO member the assembly check: made full form at the maximum-material limit, it verifies size and form together exactly as the mating part would, and it must enter or assemble. The NO-GO member guards the least-material limit dimension by dimension, point by point, and must not enter. Fixed-limit gauging trades information for speed — a verdict at production pace, but no number to trend, which is variable gauging's territory.
